title: Tiny House Dollhouse for 12" Dolls
summary: A rolling, single-room tiny-house dollhouse scaled for fashion dolls, with angled walls, cutout windows, and a fold-out convertible couch.
description:
The whole exterior geometry comes from one repeated angle — every wall is cut at 15° off square on a compound miter saw — which is what gives this its tiny-house silhouette instead of a plain rectangular box. Cut all the wall pieces to that same 15° setting in one pass rather than resetting the saw between pieces; consistency here is what lets the walls actually meet cleanly at the corners.

There's one real design decision buried in step 9 that has to be made before step 3, not after: the convertible couch/bed is sized to match a 1x12's actual width (11-1/4"), which lets it turn either direction in the living room, but a 12"-scale doll is roughly 11-1/2" wide — tight enough that some builders prefer a 12"-wide living room and a 12"-long couch instead, accepting that the couch then can't be placed against the end wall. Decide which trade-off you want and use that same width for both the inner wall spacing in step 3 and the couch cut list in step 9, or the two won't match. The back-deck railing is genuinely fussy to nail — hot glue is the honest answer for those small 1x1 pieces, not a shortcut.

tools.note: For the front angled tongue, the doorway cutout, and window openings.
Set to 15° off square for every wall cut — a compound miter saw, not a basic chop saw.

materials.name: Pine, 1x1, 3 ft
materials.note: Back deck railing — hot glue is the practical way to join these small pieces.

materials.name: Wheels, 4" diameter
materials.note: 1/2" plywood rounds work, or store-bought wheels.

materials.name: Hot glue
materials.note: For the small back-deck railing pieces.

materials.name: Bolts and washers
materials.note: Mounts the wheels to their supports.

cutList.note: Front angled tongue cut with circular saw or jigsaw.
Short-point measurement, one end cut at 15° off square.
Short-point measurement, one end cut at 15° off square.
Confirm the couch-width decision (step 9) before cutting this to final length.
Or store-bought wheels/wood rounds — size adjustable.
Use 12" instead of 11-1/4" if you chose the wider living-room option in step 9.

steps.title: Cut the bottom
steps.body: Cut the bottom piece from the 1x12, using a circular saw or jigsaw to cut the angled front tongue.

steps.title: Cut and attach the outer walls
steps.body: Cut the four outer wall pieces on a compound miter saw set to 15° off square. Cut the doorway out of one wall with a jigsaw and sand any rough edges. Attach the walls to the bottom 1x12 with 2" self-tapping screws and glue.

steps.title: Decide the couch width, then cut inner walls and loft
steps.body: IMPORTANT: read the couch sizing note in the build-the-couch step before proceeding here — it affects this step's dimensions. Cut the inner walls on the miter saw at 15° off square, sized to whichever living-room width you settled on (11-1/4" to match a 1x12, or 12" for a better doll fit). Cut the two loft-area pieces on the compound miter saw with square cuts this time. Attach with 2" self-tapping screws.

steps.title: Cut and attach the back plywood
steps.body: Cut the back plywood panel with a circular saw or table saw. Mark out the windows and drill starter holes with a bit larger than your jigsaw blade, then cut the windows out — the vertical lines between panes aren't necessary and are easier to skip. Attach the back panel with 3/4" brad nails.

steps.title: Attach the wheel supports
steps.body: Attach the wheel supports to the bottom with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket screws.

steps.title: Cut and mount the wheels
steps.body: Cut the wheels from scrap material, or use store-bought wood rounds or wheels. Drill holes and bolt each wheel to its support. Once the wheels are on, cut a 1x1 or dowel piece to length and screw it to the front tongue so the whole house sits level.

steps.title: Build the back deck railing
steps.body: Build the back-deck railing from 1x1 material. These pieces are genuinely difficult to nail or screw — hot glue is the practical way to assemble them.

steps.title: Build the convertible couch/bed
steps.body: The couch is designed at 11-1/4" to match a 1x12's actual width, letting it turn either direction in the living room — but a 12" fashion doll is closer to 11-1/2" wide, so you may prefer a 12"-wide living room and a 12"-long couch instead. That trade-off means the couch can no longer sit against the end wall, so decide before you cut the inner walls in the earlier step and use the same width both places. Build the couch by gluing and nailing the legs to the 2x4 base, then gluing and nailing the 1x2 top to the 1x4 seat. Join the two assemblies with a small hinge to make it fold flat.

steps.title: Finish and furnish
steps.body: Sand every edge, then paint or finish as you like. Additional furniture plans for the rest of the house are available separately if you'd like to fully furnish it.

images.alt: Rolling wooden tiny-house dollhouse scaled for 12-inch fashion dolls
